commands.review_summary:
The PR updates the browser plugin so Playwright-backed labeled screenshots honor full-page/ref/element scopes, adds optional per-ref `annotations` response metadata, and updates docs/tests for the new labeled-screenshot behavior.

PR surface: Source +408, Tests +539, Docs +23. Total +970 across 12 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source: current main's labeled screenshot route does not pass `fullPage`, `ref`, or `element` into the labeled Playwright helper, while the PR body also supplies after-fix live commands and JSON summaries. I did not run the browser flow locally because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A mechanical docs fix remains, but the blocking action is maintainer acceptance of the scoped-label compatibility change plus a clean rebase, so this should stay in human review rather than an automated repair lane.

target.title: feat(browser): extend --labels overlay to full-page and element captures
target.body: Bring extensions/browser `--labels` screenshot to its full intended scope: full-page captures, element/ref captures, and a per-ref bounding-box metadata array — while preserving the shipped `labelsSkipped` response contract. - `--labels --full-page` now actually projects labels across the entire page (previously silently dropped fullPage in the Playwright path). - `--labels --ref ` and `--labels --element ` now clip the capture and project labels relative to the element top-left (previously ref/element were silently dropped in the labels path). - Response includes a new `annotations` array of per-ref bounding boxes (`{ref, number, role, name?, box: {x,y,width,height}}`) so agents can reason about positions without re-snapshotting. The field is omitted when empty. - `labelsSkipped` keeps its shipped meaning: in viewport capture, refs outside the captured viewport are counted as skipped (not silently re-interpreted). `labelsCount` reports the labels actually drawn on the image; `annotations` is an additive superset that also includes off-viewport refs with their boxes. Implementation: - New `screenshot-annotate.ts` pure helper: `refToNumber`, `planAnnotations` with viewport/fullpage/element coordinate projection (with overlap filter for element mode), `buildOverlayInjectionScript` and `buildOverlayClearScript` IIFE generators. Zero Playwright dependency so the helper is reusable for the raw-CDP path in a follow-up. - `screenshotWithLabelsViaPlaywright` rewritten to consume the helper. It reads scroll + viewport size in one `page.evaluate`; in viewport capture mode, refs whose document box falls outside the viewport rect are counted as skipped and surfaced in `annotations` but not drawn (and do not consume the label budget). `labels` returns the drawn overlay count. - `/screenshot` Playwright branch forwards fullPage/ref/element through to the helper and returns annotations. - `/snapshot` Playwright branch returns annotations only (snapshot remains intentionally viewport-only and does not gain new params). - LLM-facing safeDetails surfaces annotations to the agent. Behavior summary: - `labelsSkipped` (viewport): preserved — off-viewport refs raise the count, same as shipped `main`. `labelsCount` (viewport): drawn-in-viewport labels (matches shipped `main`). New `annotations` array is additive. - Existing fullPage + (ref|element) 400 validation is unchanged. chrome-mcp and raw-CDP paths are deliberately unchanged in this change. existing-session profiles continue to omit the `annotations` field. That is documented as a known downgrade and tracked for follow-up. Tests: - `screenshot-annotate.test.ts` (31 cases): `refToNumber` forms; three coordinate-space projections; element-mode overlap filter; maxLabels truncation; empty-name omission; IIFE script content/idempotency; and viewport off-screen accounting (off-viewport counted as skipped, still surfaced in annotations, only when viewport size is supplied). - `pw-tools-core.annotate.test.ts` (7 cases): all three capture modes, finally-cleanup-on-error, off-viewport-counted-as-skipped, and bbox-failure accounting. - All existing extensions/browser tests still pass. ## Review follow-ups (ClawSweeper) This revision addresses both findings from the review: - **[P1] `labelsSkipped` semantics** — Resolved by preserving the shipped contract instead of narrowing it. In viewport capture, off-viewport refs are counted as skipped again (matching `main`); `labelsCount` reports drawn-in-viewport labels; the new `annotations` array is purely additive (it includes off-viewport refs with their boxes so agents can still locate them). No existing consumer of `labelsSkipped` is silently re-interpreted, so no maintainer compatibility decision is required. - **[P2] raw-CDP labels caveat** — Resolved. The browser-automation skill now states that the raw-CDP fallback (no Playwright) does not support labeled screenshots at all (returns 501); only chrome-mcp/existing-session renders the overlay without `annotations`. - **[P1] driver-scoped label docs** — Resolved. - **[P1] scoped capture compatibility** — Contributor chooses Accept scoped-label behavior; see Compatibility decision. No opt-in API. `docs/cli/browser.md`, `docs/tools/browser-control.md`, and the browser automation skill now scope full-page/ref/element label projection and `annotations` to Playwright-backed profiles, describe existing-session/chrome-mcp as overlay-only without `annotations`, and keep raw-CDP documented as labels-unsupported without Playwright. ## Summary What problem does this PR solve? The Playwright path of `extensions/browser` `--labels` screenshot silently dropped three caller-supplied parameters: `fullPage`, `ref`, and `element`. A user passing `--labels --full-page` got a viewport-only capture with no error. A user passing `--labels --ref e3` got a viewport capture of the whole page, ignoring the element scoping. The labels response also gave the LLM no way to reason about a ref's on-image position without re-running snapshot, so multimodal agents had to re-snapshot for every layout question. Why does this matter now? The `--labels` flag is the workhorse for vision-capable agents working on long pages or single components. The silent-drop made any prompt-engineered combination such as "screenshot the form with labels" produce wrong-but-not-erroring output, which is a worse failure mode than a clear 4xx. Adding the `annotations` array is the single biggest information-density gain for that prompt path: positions become a few hundred bytes of structured JSON instead of a re-issued snapshot RPC. What is the intended outcome? - `--labels --full-page` produces a full-page PNG/JPG with labels in document coordinates, all the way to the bottom of the page. - `--labels --ref ` and `--labels --element ` produce an element-clip PNG with labels projected relative to the element top-left, only including refs that overlap the element rect. - Every Playwright-path labels response includes `annotations: [{ref, number, role, name?, box}]` (omitted when empty). - `labelsSkipped` and `labelsCount` keep their shipped meaning for viewport captures; `annotations` is a new additive field. - A pure helper (`screenshot-annotate.ts`) holds all coordinate math and DOM-injection IIFE strings, so the same logic can be reused by the raw-CDP path later without refactor. What is intentionally out of scope? - chrome-mcp path. `existing-session` profiles continue to use `renderChromeMcpLabels` and continue to omit the `annotations` field; this is a documented downgrade. - Raw-CDP fallback. Labels require Playwright; without it the route returns 501 (was already the case). - The `snapshot` route does not gain `fullPage / ref / element` parameters; the accompanying screenshot stays viewport-only. - No new CLI flag. `--labels` keeps its current shape; only the help text and the response payload were updated. - No `CHANGELOG.md` edits (per CONTRIBUTING.md). What does success look like? - Full page (`https://playwright.dev/`, ~4121px tall) with `--labels --full-page`: every interactive ref is drawn and annotated in document coordinates, `labelsSkipped: 0`, and after capture `document.querySelectorAll('[data-openclaw-labels]').length === 0` (no DOM residue). - Same page with viewport `--labels`: only refs inside the viewport are drawn; refs below the fold are counted in `labelsSkipped` (preserving the shipped contract) and still listed in `annotations` so an agent can scroll to them. What should reviewers focus on? - Coordinate-space correctness: `planAnnotations` projects `box` relative to viewport scroll for `viewport`, returns doc-space for `fullpage`, and projects relative to `elementRect` (with an overlap filter) for `element`. Tests in `screenshot-annotate.test.ts` lock each axis. - `labelsSkipped` preservation: in viewport mode an off-viewport ref raises `labelsSkipped`, is not drawn, and is still present in `annotations`. `labelsCount` counts drawn labels. `labelsCount + labelsSkipped` stays disjoint and equals the total resolved refs, matching shipped `main`. - Cleanup invariant: `buildOverlayClearScript()` MUST run even when `page.screenshot()` throws. Covered by `pw-tools-core.annotate.test.ts` with a screenshot mock that throws. ## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes # (No tracking issue exists. The behavior gaps were discovered while building a multimodal browser agent prompt and the `--labels` family is the natural seam for the fix.) Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # (None I am aware of. If a maintainer points to one I will link it.) Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No. Drive-by contribution. The change is self-contained: deleting the new helper file and reverting `screenshotWithLabelsViaPlaywright` removes the feature cleanly. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: - `--labels --full-page` was producing viewport-only output (silently dropping `fullPage`). - `--labels --ref ` / `--labels --element ` were producing whole-page viewport output (silently dropping the scope). - `--labels` responses had no per-ref bounding box data; agents had to re-snapshot to learn positions. - Review follow-up: `labelsSkipped` must keep its shipped meaning (off-viewport refs counted as skipped) rather than being narrowed. - Real environment tested: - OS: Linux 6.8.0-101-generic x86_64 - Node: v24.16.0 - Browser: headless Chromium (Playwright-bundled), driver `openclaw`, `cdpPort 9222` - Profile: `--profile dev` running `pnpm dev --profile dev gateway run` (source mode, freshly-built code paths) - Target page: `https://playwright.dev/` (~4121px tall scrollable document) - Head: `20c9435b5ea024c62cf38db638898787a0e1ea99` (current HEAD; all evidence below was re-captured against this SHA after rebasing on latest `main`) - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ``` alias oc='node openclaw.mjs --profile dev' oc browser open https://playwright.dev --label pa # Case 1: snapshot with annotations (viewport-only accompanying screenshot) oc browser --json snapshot --target-id pa --interactive --labels # Case 2: viewport screenshot with annotations oc browser --json screenshot pa --labels # Case 3: full-page screenshot with annotations oc browser --json screenshot pa --labels --full-page # Case 4: element-clip screenshot with annotations oc browser --json screenshot pa --labels --element "nav" ``` - Evidence after fix (live JSON summaries, copied from the runs at HEAD `88942b1097`): | # | Command | labelsCount | labelsSkipped | annotations | max box.y | |---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:| | 1 | snapshot --interactive --labels (viewport) | 11 | 32 | 43 | 4121 | | 2 | screenshot --labels (viewport) | 13 | 51 | 64 | 4121 | | 3 | screenshot --labels --full-page | 64 | 0 | 64 | 1928 | | 4 | screenshot --labels --element "nav" | 4 | 0 | 4 | 15 | Reading of the numbers: - **`labelsSkipped` is preserved**: viewport captures (rows 1, 2) report 32 and 51 off-viewport refs as skipped — exactly the shipped behavior the review asked to keep. The pre-fix revision of this PR reported `labelsSkipped: 0` here. - **`annotations` is additive**: rows 1, 2 still surface all 43 / 64 resolved refs (including off-viewport ones, whose `box.y` reaches 4121 — outside the viewport image), so an agent can locate and scroll to them. - **Disjoint and consistent**: `labelsCount + labelsSkipped` equals the resolved-ref total in every row (11+32=43, 13+51=64, 64+0=64, 4+0=4). - **Full-page (row 3)**: the whole document is captured, so nothing is off-viewport: `labelsSkipped: 0`, all 64 drawn, and `max(box.y)=1928 ≤ resized image height (~2000)`. Visual captures from `https://playwright.dev/` (overlay/clipping behavior, unchanged by the `labelsSkipped` fix): **Viewport mode** (`screenshot --labels`): **Full-page mode** (`screenshot --labels --full-page`) — labels span the entire scrollable document: **Element-clip mode** (`screenshot --labels --element/--ref`) — capture clipped to the element rect; labels projected relative to the element top-left: - Observed result after fix: - `--labels --full-page` now returns a full-page labeled screenshot instead of silently falling back to the viewport-only capture. - `--labels --ref ` and `--labels --element ` now return element-clipped labeled screenshots with label boxes projected relative to the clipped image. - Viewport labeled captures preserve the shipped `labelsSkipped` behavior: off-viewport refs are counted as skipped, while `annotations` still lists the resolved refs and boxes for agent positioning. - The live JSON summaries above show the intended after-fix counts for viewport, snapshot, full-page, and element-clip modes. - What was not tested: - chrome-mcp path on `existing-session` profile. Code review confirms the labels branch routes to `renderChromeMcpLabels` first; `annotations` is intentionally absent there. - Raw-CDP fallback. Code review confirms `--labels` requires Playwright and returns 501 otherwise. - DOM cleanup was not re-captured live this round (the `evaluate` CLI rejects a label-form `--target-id` in this flow); the `finally`-clear invariant is locked by the `pw-tools-core.annotate.test.ts` "runs the clear script even when screenshot throws" case. - macOS / Windows. Linux only; no OS-specific code paths touched. - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Headless mode only. Headed-mode behavior should be identical (same `page.screenshot()` / `locator.screenshot()` calls). - Before evidence: - Before this fix on `main`, `--labels --full-page` produced a viewport-only PNG with no labels below the fold, and `--labels --ref/--element` ignored the scope. The response had no `annotations` field. The pre-fix revision of this PR additionally reported `labelsSkipped: 0` for viewport captures (the narrowing this revision reverts). ## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? ``` pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/screenshot-annotate.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.annotate.test.ts pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/client.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts pnpm tsgo:extensions pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main ``` All green. The two annotate suites report `38 passed`; the labels-touching route/tool suites report `92 passed`. `check:changed` (typecheck extensions + extension tests + extension lint + guards) passes against latest `main`. What regression coverage was added or updated? - `screenshot-annotate.test.ts`: added a "viewport off-screen accounting" group — off-viewport refs are counted as skipped and still surfaced in `annotations` when viewport size is supplied, and not counted when it is omitted (pure-helper default). - `pw-tools-core.annotate.test.ts`: the former "off-viewport not filtered" case is now "off-viewport counted as skipped but still surfaced in annotations" (asserts `skipped`, `labels`, and `annotations`). ## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, additive and compatibility-preserving: - `--labels --full-page` now produces full-page output (was silently viewport-only). - `--labels --ref`/`--element` now produce element-clip output (was silently viewport-only). - New `annotations` array on Playwright-path labels responses. - `labelsSkipped` and `labelsCount` keep their shipped meaning for viewport captures (off-viewport refs counted as skipped; drawn count reported), so existing consumers are not silently re-interpreted. Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No. No config schema changes, no migrations, no new env vars, no plugin SDK surface changes. Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No. No new permissions, no SSRF policy changes, no new network destinations, no secret-surface changes, no `evaluate`/`act` changes. What is the highest-risk area? Coordinate-space invariants in `planAnnotations` (viewport vs fullpage vs element). A bug means agents reason about the wrong screen pixel. Mitigated by the three projection tests plus the live proof above. Secondary risk — DOM residue if cleanup fails — is mitigated by the unconditional `finally { clearScript }` block and the cleanup-on-error test. ## Compatibility decision (ClawSweeper P1) Contributor position: **Accept scoped-label behavior** (ClawSweeper maintainer option 1). No opt-in `labelsScope` / `labelsScoped` flag. **Shipped behavior (main):** On the Playwright labeled screenshot path, `fullPage`, `ref`, and `element` were not forwarded to `screenshotWithLabelsViaPlaywright`. Combinations such as `--labels --full-page` returned HTTP 200 with a **viewport-only** labeled capture while silently ignoring the scope flags. The unlabeled screenshot path already honored those flags. **New behavior (this PR):** Labeled Playwright screenshots honor caller-supplied `fullPage` / `ref` / `element`, matching the unlabeled path, CLI help, and the intended `--labels` contract. **Breaking surface:** Callers that passed `labels` together with `fullPage` / `ref` / `element` and relied on the old viewport-only labeled output. This is narrower than a general screenshot API break: chrome-mcp, raw-CDP, and `snapshot --labels` scope are unchanged. **Why acceptable:** The prior behavior was a silent bug (wrong-but-not-erroring output). Honoring scope aligns labeled and unlabeled paths and matches documented CLI semantics. `labelsSkipped` / `labelsCount` keep their shipped meaning for viewport captures; `annotations` is additive. **Mitigations in this PR:** - `labelsSkipped` semantics preserved (commit `88942b1`). - Driver differences documented in `docs/cli/browser.md`, `docs/tools/browser-control.md`, and the browser automation skill. - Docs note that prior releases ignored scope on labeled Playwright screenshots (commit `771af62c70`). - P3 docs indent fixed (`docs/cli/browser.md` annotation schema line). **Request to maintainers:** Please confirm acceptance of scoped-label behavior as an intended bug fix so this PR can land without a compatibility opt-in shim. | Scenario | main | this PR | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | `screenshot --labels` | viewport + off-screen skipped | same + `annotations` | compatible | | `screenshot --labels --full-page` | viewport (scope ignored) | full-page | intentional fix | | `screenshot --labels --element nav` | viewport (scope ignored) | element clip | intentional fix | | `snapshot --labels` | viewport | viewport + `annotations` | compatible | ## Current review state What is the next action? Maintainer review and explicit acceptance of scoped-label behavior (Compatibility decision). Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? - ClawSweeper [P1] `labelsSkipped` semantics: addressed by preserving the shipped contract (see Review follow-ups + Real behavior proof rows 1-3). - ClawSweeper [P2] raw-CDP labels caveat: addressed in `extensions/browser/skills/browser-automation/SKILL.md`. - ClawSweeper [P1] driver-scoped label docs: addressed across `docs/cli/browser.md`, `docs/tools/browser-control.md`, and `extensions/browser/skills/browser-automation/SKILL.md`. - ClawSweeper [P1] scoped capture compatibility: contributor documents Accept scoped-label behavior; awaiting maintainer confirmation. - ClawSweeper [P3] docs annotation schema indent: fixed in `771af62c70`.

target.title: feat(gateway): add opt-in WebChat OpenResponses progress event
target.body:
## Summary

- Add an opt-in WebChat-only OpenResponses progress SSE event for #85702.
- Keep `response.output_text.delta` reserved for real assistant text and the existing post-run fallback.
- Document the new config and event shape so clients can render early active state without treating progress as transcript text.

## Behavior

When `gateway.webchat.openResponsesProgress.mode` is set to `"event"` and a streaming `/v1/responses` request resolves to the WebChat channel, Gateway emits:

```json
{
  "type": "response.openclaw_progress",
  "response_id": "resp_...",
  "message": "OpenClaw is working on your request.",
  "created_at": 1780000000
}
```

The event is emitted after `response.in_progress` and before assistant text deltas. It is OpenClaw-specific metadata, not assistant transcript content. The default remains off, and non-WebChat OpenResponses clients do not receive the event.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/config-misc.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 1 file, 89 tests passed.
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 2 files, 40 tests passed.
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-corepack corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-corepack corepack pnpm -s tsgo:core`

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Streaming WebChat OpenResponses clients receive an early non-transcript progress signal before assistant text is available, while non-WebChat streams keep the previous event sequence.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw Gateway process from this PR worktree at `3753b7e62d153ddb4092cb85b93edb910dd2fd13`, Node `v22.20.0`, pnpm `11.2.2`, config `gateway.webchat.openResponsesProgress.mode="event"`, real Codex app-server model turn through Gateway.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-corepack \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-88048-state \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr-88048-state/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_GMAIL_WATCHER=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CANVAS_HOST=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_ACPX_RUNTIME=1 \
OPENCLAW_SKIP_ACPX_RUNTIME_PROBE=1 \
OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1 \
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway --port 18889 --verbose

curl -sS -N http://127.0.0.1:18889/v1/responses \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <local proof token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-openclaw-message-channel: webchat' \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","stream":true,"max_output_tokens":20,"input":"Reply with exactly: proof-ok"}'

curl -m 15 -sS -N http://127.0.0.1:18889/v1/responses \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <local proof token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-openclaw-message-channel: slack' \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","stream":true,"max_output_tokens":10,"input":"Reply with exactly: off-ok"}'
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
event: response.in_progress
data: {"type":"response.in_progress",...}

event: response.openclaw_progress
data: {"type":"response.openclaw_progress","response_id":"resp_62fbe106-0339-410e-b5c7-54d828305dc4","message":"OpenClaw is working on your request.",...}

event: response.output_item.added
data: {"type":"response.output_item.added",...}

event: response.content_part.added
data: {"type":"response.content_part.added",...}

event: response.output_text.delta
data: {"type":"response.output_text.delta",...,"delta":"proof-ok"}

event: response.completed
data: {"type":"response.completed","response":{"status":"completed","output":[{"content":[{"text":"proof-ok"}]}],...}}
```

Non-WebChat negative proof:

```text
event: response.in_progress
data: {"type":"response.in_progress",...}

event: response.output_item.added
data: {"type":"response.output_item.added",...}

event: response.content_part.added
data: {"type":"response.content_part.added",...}

event: response.output_text.delta
data: {"type":"response.output_text.delta",...,"delta":"off-ok"}
```

Observed result after fix: The WebChat stream emitted `response.openclaw_progress` after `response.in_progress` and before any assistant delta; the real model then returned `proof-ok`. The non-WebChat `slack` stream did not emit `response.openclaw_progress` and returned `off-ok`.

What was not tested: Browser-rendered WebChat screenshot/video; this proof exercises the live Gateway HTTP SSE path directly.

Fixes #85702

target.title: fix(embedded-agent): treat stale thinking-block signature as replay-invalid
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #88020.

When a session with extended thinking runs long enough that early thinking-block signatures expire, Anthropic rejects the next API call with `Invalid signature in thinking block`. `REPLAY_INVALID_RE` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.ts` did not match that wording, so `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind` returned a non-replay kind and the session hard-failed instead of routing to replay recovery (strip the stale thinking blocks and retry).

Add an `\binvalid signature\b.*?\bthinking block\b` alternation to `REPLAY_INVALID_RE` so the message classifies as `replay_invalid` and the existing recovery path runs.

This is a generic product rule (matches the provider error class, not a hardcoded one-off string); the regex is intentionally scoped to the signature/thinking-block pairing to avoid false positives.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.test.ts` — 6 passed (incl. 2 new cases for the bare phrase and the `messages.N.content.M:` framed variant).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Anthropic `Invalid signature in thinking block` now classifies as `replay_invalid` so the session recovers (strip stale thinking + retry) instead of hard-failing.
Real environment tested: Unit-level via the project test runner (Vitest) against `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind`; no paid live Anthropic call.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.test.ts`
Evidence after fix: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 6 passed (6)`; new assertions `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind("Invalid signature in thinking block") === "replay_invalid"` and the framed-variant both pass.
Observed result after fix: classifier returns `replay_invalid` for the stale-signature message; prior behavior returned a non-replay kind (no recovery).
What was not tested: a full live long-session reproduction against the Anthropic API (would require an expired-signature session); the change is confined to the error-classification regex and is covered by the unit assertions.

target.title: feat(hooks): emit session:aborted + opt-in auto-continue hook
target.body:
## Summary

When the stuck-session watchdog hard-aborts an embedded run, the run goes silent — all queued progress is lost and the agent never resumes on its own. This adds a self-healing backstop:

1. **Emit `session:aborted`** — when `recoverStuckDiagnosticSession` actually aborts a run, it now fires an internal `session:aborted` hook event with the session id, age, lane, and `reason: "stuck_recovery"`.

2. **Opt-in `auto-continue` bundled hook** — on `session:aborted`, re-injects a short continuation instruction and kicks a fresh turn so the agent resumes its work loop instead of going dark. A loop-guard caps resumes at 3 per session per 30 min so a pathological abort→continue→abort cycle can't run away.

Because resuming a run automatically is an opinionated behavior change, the hook is **disabled by default**. To let a bundled hook ship disabled-by-default without weakening the workspace trust boundary, this adds a declarative `defaultEnableMode` metadata field. Enable with:

    openclaw hooks enable auto-continue

This is purely a backstop behind the existing false-abort *prevention* (working runs already refresh the watchdog activity timestamp on every run event via the ACP projector's `onProgress`). Auto-continue only matters for a genuine hang.

## Review follow-up (ClawSweeper + @joelnishanth)

Both reviewers flagged that the original `defaultEnableMode` override let **any** hook source's frontmatter loosen its enable policy — a workspace hook could declare `defaultEnableMode: "default-on"` and self-enable, bypassing the explicit-opt-in boundary that exists because workspace hooks run untrusted local code. Addressed:

1. **Trust boundary closed.** The hook source policy table now carries an explicit `allowMetadataEnableOverride` flag. Bundled / plugin / managed sources (trusted; ship with or are installed into the binary) may honor frontmatter `defaultEnableMode`; **`openclaw-workspace` is `false`** and always falls back to its source policy (explicit opt-in) regardless of what its `HOOK.md` declares. The trust decision is declarative and per-source rather than a hard-coded string check.

2. **Structured budget log.** The auto-continue loop-guard now emits a structured `auto-continue` subsystem log event (with `sessionKey`, `maxContinues`, `windowMinutes`) when the per-session budget is exhausted, instead of `console.warn` — so operators can observe the cap in gateway logs (per @joelnishanth's suggestion).

3. **Recovery + loop-guard coverage** added (see test plan).

## Real behavior proof

Tests run on this branch (`af8804e3`), Node v22, vitest 4.1.7:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/hooks/policy.test.ts \
    src/hooks/bundled/auto-continue/handler.test.ts \
    src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/.../openclaw

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  37 passed (37)
   Duration  866ms
```

```
$ pnpm tsgo:core        # core typecheck — no errors
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test   # test typecheck — no errors
$ npx oxlint src/hooks/ # exit 0
```

Behavior the new tests pin down:

- `session:aborted` fires **only** on a real abort outcome, **not** on a lane-release / noop recovery.
- A `openclaw-workspace` hook with `defaultEnableMode: "default-on"` in metadata stays **disabled** (`reason: "workspace hook (disabled by default)"`).
- A trusted source (bundled/managed) **can** set a stricter `explicit-opt-in` default via metadata.
- The auto-continue loop-guard queues a continuation + heartbeat wake on abort, stops after 3 resumes in the 30-min window, and tracks the budget independently per session key.

Live gateway context that motivated the patch (OpenClaw 2026.5.20, systemd user service): a genuinely stuck embedded run was aborted by the watchdog at ~367s and went silent — no further output reached the chat channel. With `auto-continue` enabled, the same abort now re-injects the continuation and the agent resumes its work loop on its own.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` — no errors
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — no errors
- [x] `vitest run` on the 3 affected files — 37/37 pass (policy boundary, session:aborted emission, auto-continue loop-guard)
- [x] `oxlint src/hooks/` — clean

Allow edits by maintainers: yes.

The branch changes `runSecretsApply` to stage satellite credential writes through `writeTextFileAtomic`, commit config before renaming staged files, and adds two write-fault regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. from source and supplied proof: current main commits config before the satellite write loop and restores only best-effort, and the PR body shows a live injected ENOSPC probe diverging before the fix. I did not execute the repro because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining action is secops/maintainer review of credential-source semantics and the disclosed crash window; no narrow automated repair is indicated.

target.title: fix(secrets): stage all files then commit so apply never half-migrates
target.body:
# fix(secrets): stage all files then commit so apply never half-migrates

> AI-assisted PR. Drafted with Claude Code (claude-opus-4-8). Fully tested: a new regression test in `src/secrets/apply.test.ts` fails before this change and passes after, and the fix was verified with a live before/after real-behavior proof (see below). Author has reviewed and understands the change.

## 1. Summary

- **Problem**: `runSecretsApply` migrates plaintext credentials to `SecretRef`s across `openclaw.json` plus N satellite files (`auth-profiles.json` / legacy `auth.json` / `.env`) with no cross-file transaction boundary. A fault partway through the commit leaves config and the auth-stores diverged.
- **Why it matters**: The satellite files are the credential source-of-truth (OAuth / api_key tokens). A divergence means either plaintext credentials left un-scrubbed on disk (leak surface) or config refs that no longer resolve (credentials fail to load). The existing rollback is best-effort and the same fault (disk full / EIO) that aborts the commit also defeats the restore, so the divergence becomes permanent.
- **What changed**: The commit block is restructured into a two-phase stage-then-commit. Phase A writes every satellite file's final content to a temp file in its own directory (through the same private-store write the live commit uses); if any staging throws, the temps are discarded and the apply rethrows without touching a single live file. Phase B commits config first via `replaceConfigFile`, then renames the staged temps into place (cheap metadata ops). Both fault-prone writes (the satellite content writes in Phase A and the config write in Phase B) now happen before any live satellite file advances, so a write fault on either leaves every store un-migrated.
- **What did NOT change**: No public API / contract change. `runSecretsApply`'s signature is unchanged, no new exports, no behavior change on the healthy (no-fault) path, and `replaceConfigFile` is still the config writer. The legacy best-effort snapshot rollback is retained for the now-rare Phase B failure path.

## 2. Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Docs / CI only

## 3. Scope

Touched areas:
- [x] `src/secrets/` (secrets apply commit path)

Files:
- `src/secrets/apply.ts` (+92 / -2): two-phase commit block (config committed before satellite renames), `stageWrite` / `commitStaged` / `discardStaged` helpers.
- `src/secrets/apply.test.ts` (+181): two new regression tests (satellite-write fault, config-write fault).

## 4. Linked Issue

Closes #88012

## 5. Root Cause

`runSecretsApply`'s write-mode commit block committed `openclaw.json` first via `replaceConfigFile`, then wrote the satellite credential files in a plain `for` loop of `writeTextFileAtomic` with **no cross-file transaction, journal, or single lock boundary**. Each file write is individually atomic (temp + rename), but there is no boundary spanning the set. If a satellite write faulted mid-loop (ENOSPC, EACCES from a `0o700` parent mkdir, or EIO) after config and earlier stores had already committed, config was migrated to refs while some auth-stores still held plaintext.

The missing guardrail: the `catch` block rolls back **best-effort only** (`restoreFileSnapshot` calls `writeTextFileAtomic` inside a `try/catch` that swallows failures, commented "Best effort only"). The fault that caused the throw (disk full / IO error) also fails the restore write, so the partial migration persists. There was no point in the flow where the fault-prone writes were forced to complete before any live file advanced.

## 6. Regression Test Plan

Two new tests in `src/secrets/apply.test.ts`, one per fault-prone write covered by the two-phase commit:

1. `"does not leave config and auth-store diverged when a satellite write faults mid-commit"` (the satellite write, Phase A):
   - Seeds a second agent auth-store with a plaintext api_key alongside config + the main store, so the commit touches >= 2 satellite files and opens a partial-commit window.
   - Injects a deterministic, sticky ENOSPC at the OS write primitive (`fs.writeFileSync`) scoped to the second auth-store's directory. The same fault also defeats the best-effort restore, reproducing the production failure mode. A real read-only parent dir does not work here because the private file store chmods the parent back to its dirMode before every write.
   - Asserts all-or-nothing: `config migrated => the faulting store also migrated`, and `faulting store still plaintext => config rolled back`. The diverged state (`config=ref AND store=plaintext`) must never persist.
2. `"does not leave config and auth-store diverged when the final config write faults after staging"` (the config write, Phase B):
   - Lets satellite staging succeed, then injects a sticky ENOSPC at the async write primitive (`node:fs/promises.writeFile`) scoped to the config temp (`replaceConfigFile` -> `replaceFileAtomic`).
   - Asserts the config-write fault leaves every store un-migrated: config keeps its plaintext and no satellite is advanced. Because config is committed before any satellite rename, the fault cannot leave migrated satellites diverged against an old config.

Before this change a satellite-write fault advances config to refs while a store keeps plaintext (migrated 1/2); after, nothing advances. The config-write test guards the ordering: committing config before any rename keeps a config fault all-or-nothing.

No fixture exposes a real credential value: stub tokens use the `// pragma: allowlist secret` annotation already established in this file.

Run: `pnpm test src/secrets/apply.test.ts`. Full gate before merge: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`.

## 7. Security Impact (required)

This PR touches a **CODEOWNERS-gated security path** and handles plaintext credentials. It must be reviewed by the secrets owners before merge.

- **CODEOWNERS gate**: `.github/CODEOWNERS` assigns `/src/secrets/` to `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`. The only modified files (`src/secrets/apply.ts`, `src/secrets/apply.test.ts`) sit inside that gate, so this PR cannot merge without explicit secops review. Please notify `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`.
- **Credential source-of-truth path**: `runSecretsApply` migrates plaintext OAuth / api_key tokens into `SecretRef`s. The bug being fixed is itself a credential-safety issue: on a faulting apply, plaintext credentials could be left un-scrubbed on disk (a leak surface) or config refs could be left dangling (credentials fail to load). The fix makes the migration all-or-nothing, which is a net improvement to credential-handling safety.
- **No new permissions, no network calls, no new data exposure**: The change is purely a local filesystem commit-ordering change. Staging reuses the **same private-store write contract as the live commit**: `stageWrite` calls `writeTextFileAtomic`, which for the `0o600` credential stores routes through `@openclaw/fs-safe`'s private file store (re-creating the parent directory at `0o700`, guarding its directory identity, and writing the temp at `0o600`) before Phase B renames it into place in the same directory. No raw `fs.writeFileSync` is introduced on the credential path, so the existing private-store hardening (directory-mode re-assertion, directory-identity guard) is preserved end-to-end. No credential value is logged or added to test fixtures in plaintext.
- **Adjacent gate (informational)**: a follow-up for the concurrent-writer axis (FIND-002, `AUTH_STORE_LOCK` bypass during apply) would touch `/src/agents/auth-profiles/`, also secops-owned. It is intentionally out of scope here and will be a separate PR.

## 8. Repro + Verification

- **Environment**: macOS (darwin arm64), Node 23.x, OpenClaw checked out at base and head shas.
- **Steps**:
  1. Seed config + two agent auth-stores with plaintext credentials.
  2. Run `runSecretsApply({ write: true })` while a sticky ENOSPC is injected at the OS write primitive scoped to the second store's directory (so its commit throws and the rollback restore also throws).
  3. Read each store's final on-disk state.
- **Expected (after fix)**: all-or-nothing. Either every store + config advanced to refs, or every store + config stayed in its original state. Never `config=ref AND a store=plaintext`.
- **Actual (before fix)**: config advanced to a ref while the second store kept plaintext (migrated 1/2, diverged). The best-effort rollback could not restore because the same fault failed its write.

## 9. Evidence

Failing test before the change, passing after:

```text
# before this change (commit block as the plain for-loop)
$ pnpm test src/secrets/apply.test.ts
  x does not leave config and auth-store diverged when a satellite write faults mid-commit
    AssertionError: expected true to be false
    (configMigrated=true && failedStillPlaintext=true -> diverged persisted)

# after this change (two-phase stage-then-commit)
$ pnpm test src/secrets/apply.test.ts
  ok does not leave config and auth-store diverged when a satellite write faults mid-commit
  (all apply.test.ts cases green)
```

See the Real behavior proof section below for the live before/after measurement against the actual `runSecretsApply` (not just the unit test).

## 10. Human Verification

- Read the full diff and confirmed the two-phase ordering: Phase A stages every satellite write to a temp via `writeTextFileAtomic` (the same private-store atomic write the live commit uses); on any Phase A throw, temps are discarded and the apply rethrows with no live file touched; Phase B commits config first via `replaceConfigFile`, then renames the staged temps into place.
- Confirmed the healthy path is unaffected: the no-fault control trial migrates all stores + config exactly as before (verified in the real-behavior proof control trial).
- Confirmed no public contract change: `runSecretsApply` signature unchanged, no new exports. `stageWrite` reuses `writeTextFileAtomic` from `shared.ts` (the same primitive `restoreFileSnapshot` already uses), so staging shares the established private-store write path instead of introducing a new one.
- Confirmed no plaintext credential is written to logs or fixtures (stub tokens carry `// pragma: allowlist secret`).

## 11. Review Conversations

- [ ] secops (CODEOWNERS) review requested and addressed.
- [ ] Bot review comments (clawsweeper / Greptile) triaged and resolved.

## 12. Compatibility / Migration

No migration required. On-disk file formats, paths, modes, and the public API are unchanged. Existing data and a healthy apply behave identically; the only behavioral difference is on the fault path, where a faulting apply now leaves all stores un-migrated instead of half-migrated. No config or version bump.

## 13. Risks and Mitigations

- **Residual crash window (disclosed)**: Both fault-prone writes are now ordered before any live satellite advances (satellite content writes in Phase A, the config write at the start of Phase B), so a write fault on either leaves every store un-migrated. The only residual is a process crash *between* individual Phase B renames, which could leave a subset renamed. Renames are same-directory metadata ops with a fault window orders of magnitude smaller than writes, so the practical exposure is much lower than the original write-loop window. True crash-atomicity would require a single journal or directory rename, which is out of scope (the secrets files are spread across multiple directories; that is an M+ change). This is called out in the Real behavior proof "What was not tested".
- **Phase B failure rollback**: A rare Phase B failure (the config commit, which runs first, or a later rename collision) still falls back to the existing best-effort snapshot restore and discards any un-renamed temps, degrading to the original state rather than a partial commit. A config-commit fault happens before any rename, so nothing has advanced.
- **Temp file leftovers**: Temps use `process.pid` + `randomUUID()` names and are written through the private-store atomic write (exclusive `wx` create at the fs-safe layer, no clobber of a concurrent stage); `discardStaged` removes any un-committed temps in both the Phase A and Phase B failure paths.
- **CODEOWNERS / merge risk**: Cannot merge without secops review (mitigation: secops explicitly tagged in Security Impact).

---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Without this patch, runSecretsApply (src/secrets/apply.ts:835-855) commits one logical credential-migration transaction (plaintext credential -> SecretRef) as a non-atomic sequence: replaceConfigFile for openclaw.json, then a `for` loop of writeTextFileAtomic over N auth-profiles.json / legacy auth.json / .env. There is no cross-file transaction, journal, or single lock boundary. If a satellite write throws mid-loop (ENOSPC / EACCES / EIO) after earlier stores already committed, the catch block runs a best-effort rollback (restoreFileSnapshot wrapped in try/catch that swallows failures, "Best effort only"). When the same fault also fails a restore write, the partial migration persists: some auth-stores are left migrated to SecretRef while others stay plaintext, diverging the credential source-of-truth. With this patch, a mid-commit fault leaves no partial migration (all-or-nothing: full rollback or full commit).
- **Real environment tested**: macOS (darwin arm64), Node 23.x, OpenClaw worktrees checked out at the base and head shas (base = 615199a6a4, the fork-point this branch rebases onto). tsx executes src/secrets/apply.ts directly (--skip-build). Each trial builds a fresh isolated temp HOME via fs.mkdtempSync (os.tmpdir()); production ~/.openclaw and real credentials are never touched, and the temp HOME is removed after each trial. No external dependencies (no OAuth / LLM / network). The disk fault is injected deterministically through @openclaw/fs-safe's test-only DI seam (__setFsSafeTestHooksForTest.beforeFileStoreSyncPrivateWrite, gated on NODE_ENV=test) because OS permission denial is self-healed by fs-safe's ensurePrivateDirectorySync (it re-mkdirs and chmods the store directory).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
$ pnpm install --frozen-lockfile                      (both worktrees, --skip-build)
$ node_modules/.bin/tsx <probe.ts>                    (worktree-relative)
  Control trial : runSecretsApply(write) with no fault -> expect full commit, no divergence.
  Defect trial  : fault injected so store#2 commit throws AND store#1 rollback restore throws.
                  Measures each store's final on-disk state (ref / plaintext / missing).
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Live Node.js measurement of per-store final state after a mid-commit throw:

```text
[Build A] without this patch (base sha):
  control trial: stores={"store#1-alpha": "ref", "store#2-beta": "ref"} config=ref diverged=False
  defect  trial: threw=True stores={"store#1-alpha": "ref", "store#2-beta": "plaintext"} config=plaintext
                 migratedStores=1/2 diverged=True  (partial migration persisted)

[Build B] with this patch (head sha):
  control trial: stores={"store#1-alpha": "ref", "store#2-beta": "ref"} config=ref diverged=False
  defect  trial: threw=True stores={"store#1-alpha": "plaintext", "store#2-beta": "plaintext"} config=plaintext
                 migratedStores=0/2 diverged=False  (fault reached staging; every store rolled back)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Without patch, a mid-commit throw leaves migratedStores=1/2 with diverged=True (a strict non-empty subset of auth-stores migrated to SecretRef while the rest stay plaintext). With patch, the same injected fault now throws inside Phase A staging (threw=True) and yields migratedStores=0/2 with diverged=False: because staging routes through the same private-store write the live commit uses, the injected fs-safe fault reaches the new `stageWrite` path and the apply aborts before any live file is touched, leaving every store in its original (un-migrated) state. The control trial reports diverged=False/False on both builds, confirming the healthy full-commit path is not flagged.
- **What was not tested**: SECURITY-SENSITIVE PATH: src/secrets/apply.ts touches the credential source-of-truth and is adjacent to the .github/CODEOWNERS /src/agents/*auth* gate (auth-profiles store). Any fix that introduces a cross-file transaction/journal or acquires AUTH_STORE_LOCK_OPTIONS during apply commits must be reviewed under that CODEOWNERS gate before merge. Not tested here: (1) the concurrent-writer lost-update axis (FIND-secrets-apply-cross-store-consistency-002, AUTH_STORE_LOCK bypass) which is a separate child task of this epic; (2) production-scale fault timing under real ENOSPC/EIO (modeled deterministically via the fs-safe DI seam rather than real disk exhaustion); (3) the legacy auth.json and .env satellite writes (same loop, not exercised by this plan); (4) the residual window where the process crashes between individual Phase B renames is not closed (true crash-atomicity would need a single journal or directory rename, which is out of scope here).

target.title: fix(ui): treat started managed-service handoff as pending update instead of skipped
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87889.

When a user clicks "Update now" in the Dashboard on a global install with a managed-service supervisor, the Gateway starts a managed-service handoff and returns `ok=true` with `result.status="skipped"` and `reason="managed-service-handoff-started"`. The Dashboard code previously checked `status === "ok"` to decide success, so the handoff response fell through to the skipped-update banner even though the handoff was successfully started.

ClawSweeper review identified two additional issues in the initial PR; both are addressed in the latest push:

- **P2:** The regression test used a fictional `result.after.version` that does not match the real gateway managed-service handoff payload. The real response contains `status: "skipped"`, `reason: "managed-service-handoff-started"`, and optionally `before: { version }`, but no `after` field.
- **P1:** Because `after.version` was absent, `pendingUpdateExpectedVersion` became `null`, causing `verifyPendingUpdateVersion` to exit immediately and skip post-restart version verification.

## Change

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/config.ts` — `runUpdate` now treats any `ok=true` response as success/pending, regardless of `result.status`. This aligns the Dashboard with the Gateway contract: `ok=true` means either the update succeeded or the managed-service handoff was started.
- `runUpdate` now derives `pendingUpdateExpectedVersion` through a three-tier fallback: `result.after.version` -> `updateAvailable.latestVersion` -> `null`. This ensures post-restart version verification stays active even when the gateway handoff response does not include an `after` version.
- Added `managed-service-handoff-started` to the actionable guidance map so the banner text is informative if the handoff ever surfaces as an error.
- Added optional `updateAvailable` to `ConfigState` so `runUpdate` can access the latest known version when needed.

**Before:**
- Gateway returns `ok=true, result.status=skipped, reason=managed-service-handoff-started, handoff.status=started`
- Dashboard sees `status=skipped` -> shows "Update skipped" error banner
- User thinks update failed

**After:**
- Dashboard sees `ok=true` -> sets `pendingUpdateExpectedVersion` (from `after.version` or `updateAvailable.latestVersion`) and returns without banner
- Gateway restarts, UI polls update status, post-restart verification handles the rest

## Files changed

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/config.ts` — derive pending version via `after.version ?? updateAvailable.latestVersion ?? null`; add handoff guidance; add optional `updateAvailable` to `ConfigState`
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/config.test.ts` — 2 tests: regression test with real handoff payload shape (no `after`, uses `updateAvailable` fallback), plus boundary test for null-fallback when neither `after` nor `updateAvailable` is present

## Verification

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/config.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       41 passed (41)
Duration    2.45s
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Dashboard shows "Update skipped" banner when managed-service handoff is actually started (#87889).

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `fix/dashboard-handoff-skipped-state`, Linux, Node.js 22.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx -e '
import { runUpdate } from "./ui/src/ui/controllers/config.ts";
import type { ConfigState } from "./ui/src/ui/controllers/config.ts";

const state = {
  client: {
    request: async () => ({
      ok: true,
      result: {
        status: "skipped",
        reason: "managed-service-handoff-started",
        before: { version: "2026.5.0" },
      },
    }),
  },
  connected: true,
  applySessionKey: "test",
  updateRunning: false,
  lastError: null,
  chatError: null,
  updateStatusBanner: null,
  pendingUpdateExpectedVersion: null,
  updateAvailable: { latestVersion: "2026.6.1" },
} as unknown as ConfigState;

await runUpdate(state);
console.log("pendingUpdateExpectedVersion:", state.pendingUpdateExpectedVersion);
console.log("updateStatusBanner:", state.updateStatusBanner);
console.log("updateRunning:", state.updateRunning);
console.log("lastError:", state.lastError);
'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from running the patched `runUpdate` source entrypoint with the real managed-service handoff payload:

```text
pendingUpdateExpectedVersion: 2026.6.1
updateStatusBanner: null
updateRunning: false
lastError: null
```

**Observed result after fix:** When the gateway returns `ok=true` with `status=skipped` and `reason=managed-service-handoff-started`, the Dashboard no longer shows an error banner and correctly sets `pendingUpdateExpectedVersion` to `updateAvailable.latestVersion` (`2026.6.1`).

**What was not tested:** Live browser Dashboard click-through with a managed-service supervisor (the proof exercises the actual `runUpdate` source entrypoint).

Refs: #87889

target.title: fix: recognize bundle-mcp as known synthetic plugin in tool policy allowlist
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw doctor` reports `bundle-mcp` as an "unknown allowlist entry" when it is included in `tools.allow`:

```
[tools] agents.main.tools.allow allowlist contains unknown entries (bundle-mcp).
These entries won't match any tool unless the plugin is enabled.
```

This happens because `analyzeAllowlistByToolType()` only considers entries valid if they match a registered plugin ID or a core tool name. `bundle-mcp` is a synthetic plugin ID used for MCP bundled tools — it is only registered at runtime when MCP servers connect, so it is never visible during doctor's offline tool discovery pass.

## Fix

Add a `KNOWN_SYNTHETIC_PLUGIN_IDS` set (containing `"bundle-mcp"`) to the `isPluginEntry` check in `analyzeAllowlistByToolType()`, so that known synthetic plugin IDs are recognized as valid plugin entries regardless of MCP runtime state.

## Testing

- `openclaw doctor` no longer reports `bundle-mcp` as unknown
- MCP tools continue to function correctly at runtime (the allowlist expansion via `bundle-mcp` still works)
- No behavior change for other tool policy logic

target.title: fix(agents): scale read output for small contexts
target.body:
This keeps default `read` output smaller for small-context local models without changing explicit caller limits. It is part of the small-model stability work: smaller models were getting oversized file reads by default, then losing useful context or stalling around follow-up tool use.

Refs https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86599.

## Summary

- Scale the adaptive default `read` byte cap from the active model context window, with an 8KB floor and 128KB ceiling.
- Preserve explicit `limit` values, including `0` and negative values that the base read tool normalizes to one line.
- Keep continuation guidance inside the byte budget and avoid misleading offsets when the first line or trailing whitespace-only lines hit the cap.
- Add focused coverage for small-context caps, long lines, explicit limits, whitespace-only capped content, EOF pagination, and continuation-text-in-file false positives.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: small-context models get a smaller default file-read payload, while explicit caller limits keep the existing read contract.

Real environment tested: local focused node-wrapper tests for read-tool behavior; AWS Crabbox Linux changed-gate runner.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.adds-claude-style-aliases-schemas-without-dropping-g.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.arg-value-suffix.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-edit-access.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts --reporter=dot
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"
```

Evidence after fix: refreshed head `c1c0e492b5f71867a9891f1385c082cbca0a111b`; focused local wrapper passed 5 files / 41 tests; diff check passed. AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` passed with provider `aws`, lease `cbx_170dfd1a4fe3`, slug `blue-shrimp`, run `run_83d62fc1cda9`, machine `c7a.8xlarge`, exit `0`, `leaseStopped=true`, selected lanes `core` and `coreTests`.

Observed result after fix: adaptive reads cap output for small context windows, explicit limits still delegate to the base read tool, capped whitespace-only pages keep emitted content aligned with continuation offsets, and continuation-looking file text is not treated as pagination metadata.

What was not tested: real Ollama/model-provider live chat; this PR changes read-tool context sizing, not provider streaming.

target.title: fix(cron): add cron edit --clear-model to clear a job's model override
target.body:
## Summary

- The Gateway RPC `cron.update` already supports **clearing** a cron job's `payload.model` override by sending `model: null` (landed via #91298 — the patch schema, `normalizeCronJobPatch`, and `mergeCronPayload` all honor `null` as "delete this key").
- But the CLI `openclaw cron edit` has **no way to send it**: `--model` only *sets* a value (an empty string is silently ignored), and there is no `--clear-model` flag — unlike the sibling field `toolsAllow`, which has `--clear-tools`. So #91298's fix is unreachable from the CLI, the most common install/edit path, and a model override stuck on a cron job cannot be removed without hand-editing gateway state.
- This adds `--clear-model` to `cron edit`, mirroring the existing `--clear-tools` flag.

## Root cause

`src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-edit.ts` only ever assigns `model` when it is truthy:

```ts
assignIf(payload, "model", model, Boolean(model)); // empty/absent -> not sent
```

There was no clear flag, so the CLI could never produce the `model: null` patch that `mergeCronPayload` (`src/cron/service/jobs.ts`) already honors:

```ts
if (typeof patch.model === "string") next.model = patch.model;
else if (patch.model === null) delete next.model; // <- reachable from RPC, not CLI
```

`--clear-tools` already demonstrates the intended pattern for the sibling `toolsAllow` field (`payload.toolsAllow = null`).

## What changed (`src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-edit.ts`)

- Add the `--clear-model` option.
- Reject `--model` + `--clear-model` together (matches the existing `--agent`/`--clear-agent` and `--session-key`/`--clear-session-key` guards).
- Include `--clear-model` in the agentTurn-payload trigger so a clear-only edit still builds the patch.
- Emit `payload.model = null` when `--clear-model` is set (mirrors `--clear-tools` → `toolsAllow: null`).

Net `+10 / -1` in prod, `+28` test.

## Scope boundary

Only `model` is addressed: its RPC null-clear already shipped (#91298), so the CLI flag is the **sole** missing piece. Sibling agentTurn override fields (`thinking`, `fallbacks`) are not yet clearable at the RPC layer either — open PR #67429 adds that — so their CLI clear flags are the natural follow-up once #67429 lands. This change is orthogonal to #67429 (no shared files, no overlap).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Linked Issue

Source-discovered while reading the cron clear-override paths. Completes the CLI half of #91298 (model-clear); related: #67429 (RPC null-clear for `fallbacks`/`thinking`).

## Evidence

- [x] **Failing before / passing after.** New test `clears the model override with --clear-model` asserts `cron edit <id> --clear-model` sends `{ payload: { kind: "agentTurn", model: null } }`. Before this change the flag does not exist, so the parse errors with `unknown option '--clear-model'`. New help-doc test asserts `--clear-model` is documented alongside `--clear-tools`. Full file: **6/6 tests pass** (Node 22.22.1).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw cron edit <id> --clear-model` now removes a cron job's `payload.model` override (reverting the job to the agent/default model); previously there was no CLI way to clear it.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw dev gateway (`gateway run --dev --auth none`) on Node 22.22.1 (macOS), isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`/`OPENCLAW_HOME`, CLI driven via `scripts/run-node.mjs` against the live gateway RPC.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
$ openclaw cron add --every 1h --session isolated --message hi --model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 --name clearmodel-proof
$ openclaw cron edit <id> --clear-model
$ openclaw cron edit <id> --model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 --clear-model
```

Evidence after fix (live gateway output, payload read back via `cron list --json`):

```
BEFORE clear:
  payload = {"kind": "agentTurn", "message": "hi", "model": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"}

$ openclaw cron edit 4ee52523-084a-4fd9-9fe2-6864dfe2ba1a --clear-model
  (ok)
AFTER  clear:
  payload = {"kind": "agentTurn", "message": "hi"}          <- model override removed

$ openclaw cron edit 4ee52523-084a-4fd9-9fe2-6864dfe2ba1a --model X --clear-model
  Error: Use --model or --clear-model, not both             <- conflict guard
```

Observed result after fix: the `model` key is deleted from the stored cron payload (job now runs on the agent/default model); passing both `--model` and `--clear-model` is rejected.

What was not tested: a live agent run of the cleared cron job (the clearing behavior is fully observable in the persisted payload via the gateway; running the job would require a real model/provider).

## Compatibility / Migration

Backward compatible — purely additive CLI flag. No config/schema/migration changes (the Gateway already accepts `model: null` on `cron.update`).

## AI assistance

AI-assisted (Claude Code). Verified locally: 6/6 unit tests, oxlint clean, oxfmt clean, and the live before/after gateway proof above. Author understands the change.

target.title: fix: harden config schema extension id maps [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: generated config schemas wrote plugin and channel ids directly into JSON schema `properties` maps.
- Solution: normalize those dynamic schema property maps to null-prototype records and add entries with safe data-property semantics.
- What changed: added schema-map helpers for plugin/channel config schema assembly and a regression test for object-special extension ids.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): extension id normalization, config defaults, lookup path restrictions, and ordinary plugin/channel schema output shape are unchanged.

AI-assisted: yes.

## Motivation

- Generated config schema output should stay structurally stable when extension metadata contains object-special key names.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- N/A in public PR metadata.
- [x] This PR addresses a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `buildConfigSchema` safely represents plugin and channel ids that match object-special key names as own schema entries.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout using the repository Vitest wrapper for the runtime-config test project.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: `Test Files 1 passed (1)` and `Tests 45 passed (45)` for `src/config/schema.test.ts`.
- Observed result after fix: generated plugin and channel schema property maps keep a null prototype and expose `__proto__`, `constructor`, and `prototype` as own entries with reachable schema properties.
- What was not tested: full test suite, packaged build, and a live gateway run with an installed extension using those ids.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the focused regression failed before the final normalization pass because one generated plugin property map did not keep the expected null prototype.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: dynamic extension ids were inserted into ordinary JSON schema `properties` objects with bracket assignment.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression coverage for extension ids that overlap object-special key names.
- Contributing context (if known): schema merge cloning can turn a previously normalized property map back into an ordinary object, so final schema assembly also needs normalization.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/config/schema.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: plugin and channel ids `__proto__`, `constructor`, and `prototype` remain own entries in generated schema property maps without changing those maps' prototypes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: `buildConfigSchema` owns the property-map generation path, so a direct schema test covers both plugin and channel insertion without unrelated runtime setup.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Generated config schema output now safely includes plugin/channel ids matching object-special key names as own properties. No config defaults or migrations changed.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local worktree
- Runtime/container: repository Vitest wrapper in local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): generated plugin/channel config schema metadata
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Generate a config schema with plugin and channel ids named `__proto__`, `constructor`, and `prototype`.
2. Inspect the generated plugin and channel schema `properties` maps.
3. Run `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts`.

### Expected

- Dynamic schema maps keep inert prototypes and store each special id as an own schema property.

### Actual

- The focused config schema test passed with all 45 tests green.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused config schema test passed after the change; regression checks plugin and channel ids for all three object-special names.
- Edge cases checked: own-property presence, null-prototype maps, and reachable nested plugin/channel config schema properties.
- What you did **not** verify: full suite, packaging, live gateway startup, or installed third-party extension behavior.

## Review Conversations

- [x] N/A; no bot review conversations were handled in this metadata draft.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: internal code that expects these generated `properties` maps to inherit `Object.prototype` could behave differently.
  - Mitigation: schema traversal in the touched path uses static object helpers and enumerable entries; JSON serialization remains unchanged for consumers that receive the schema over the wire.

target.title: fix(cli): replace hardcoded "--" with FLAG_TERMINATOR constant in getCommandPathInternal
target.body:
## Summary

Replace the sole remaining hardcoded `"--"` literal in `getCommandPathInternal` with the `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant already imported and used by every other function in `argv.ts`.

Prevents silent subcommand-routing divergence if `FLAG_TERMINATOR` is ever changed.

**Addressed review feedback (ClawSweeper P3):** Reworded the regression test comment to accurately describe what it proves (integration with the constant), since the test cannot independently detect a literal regression while `FLAG_TERMINATOR` remains `"--"`. The companion contract test asserts the canonical value.

## Linked context

Closes #83902

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** `getCommandPathInternal` used a hardcoded `"--"` string while all other functions in the same file use the `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main (050c0813b)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
npx tsx proof-89897.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

```
=== Live Proof: #89897 / #83902 ===
FLAG_TERMINATOR constant: "--"

Case 1: getCommandPath([node, openclaw, channels, --, add], 2)
  Result: [channels]
  PASS: true

Case 2: getCommandPath([node, openclaw, channels, add], 2)
  Result: [channels, add]
  PASS: true

Case 3: getCommandPath([node, openclaw, config, --, set, --key, value], 2)
  Result: [config]
  PASS: true

Case 4: getCommandPathWithRootOptions([node, openclaw, --debug, status, --, extra], 2)
  Result: [status]
  PASS: true

Case 5: FLAG_TERMINATOR === "--"
  PASS: true

=== Live Proof #89897: ALL PASSED ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:** FLAG_TERMINATOR correctly stops command-path parsing. Command-path resolution is consistent with all other argv helpers.
- **What was not tested:** N/A — single-line refactor with focused regression tests and live CLI behavior proof.

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/cli/argv.test.ts --reporter=verbose
# 112 passed (1 test file)
```

**What regression coverage was added or updated?**
- `"uses FLAG_TERMINATOR constant to stop command-path parsing"` — exercises integration with the imported constant
- `"FLAG_TERMINATOR is the canonical -- string"` — contract test asserting the canonical value

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** No
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
**What is the highest-risk area?** None — identical runtime behavior (same constant value `"--"`).
**How is that risk mitigated?** 112 argv tests pass; the contract test explicitly asserts the canonical value.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Requesting ClawSweeper re-review.
**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** None — live CLI behavior proof added.

commands.author: whiteyzy

target.title: fix(web_fetch): strip whitespace from URLs to prevent new URL() parse errors
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91651: LLMs occasionally generate `web_fetch` URLs with accidental spaces (e.g. `"https:// docs.example.com/page"`) which cause `new URL()` to throw `Invalid URL: must be http or https`.

**Revised approach** (per ClawSweeper review): Instead of unconditionally stripping all whitespace, the fix uses try-catch fallback:
1. Try `new URL(params.url)` first — preserves standard percent-encoding for valid URLs
2. Only if parsing fails, fall back to whitespace-stripped URL — recovers from LLM-generated space typos
3. This ensures valid URLs with path/query spaces (e.g. `https://example.com/a%20b`) keep their percent-encoding instead of being silently rewritten

## Linked context

Closes #91651

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `web_fetch` tool rejects URLs with accidental internal whitespace (LLM generation artifact). Old approach was too aggressive (stripped all whitespace, breaking valid URLs). New try-catch approach preserves valid URL semantics.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main (050c0813b)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node proof-91693.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

```
=== URL Parsing Fix: try-catch fallback ===

Case 1: LLM-generated whitespace
  Input:  "https:// docs.example.com/page"
  Raw URL() throws: true
  Recovered href: https://docs.example.com/page
  PASS: true

Case 2: Tab after scheme
  Input:  "https://\texample.com/path"
  Recovered href: https://example.com/path
  PASS: true

Case 3: Valid URL with path/query spaces
  Input:  "https://example.com/a b/search?q=hello world"
  Raw URL() succeeds: true
  Result href: https://example.com/a%20b/search?q=hello%20world
  Path spaces encoded: true  Query spaces encoded: true
  PASS: true (spaces preserved as %20, NOT stripped)

Case 4: Normal URL (no-op)
  Input:  "https://example.com/normal"
  Result href: https://example.com/normal
  PASS: true

=== Live HTTP fetch with space-stripped URL ===

Malformed URL: "https:// example.com"
Cleaned URL:   "https://example.com"
HTTP status: 200
Content-length: chunked
Body preview: <!doctype html><html lang="en"><head><title>Example Domain</title>...
Live fetch: PASS

=== URL parsing: ALL PASSED ===
=== Proof #91693: COMPLETE ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Malformed URLs are recovered via whitespace stripping, while valid URLs with path/query spaces retain correct percent-encoding. Live HTTP fetch confirms the cleaned URL resolves.
- **What was not tested:** All HTTP URLs that could be affected by whitespace removal (infinite combinatorial space). The fix uses standard URL parser behavior for valid URLs.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Live fetch depends on network access; URL parsing logic is deterministic and verified across 4 edge cases.

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/web-fetch.cf-markdown.test.ts --reporter=verbose
# 9 passed (1 test file, includes new "preserves valid path/query spaces as percent-encoding" test)
```

**What regression coverage was added or updated?**
- `"recovers from URLs with accidental internal whitespace (#91651)"` — fetches URL with space after scheme, verifies cleanup
- `"preserves valid path/query spaces as percent-encoding"` — verifies valid path/query spaces are percent-encoded, NOT stripped

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — URLs with internal whitespace now succeed instead of throwing errors, while valid URLs with path/query spaces are unchanged.
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
**What is the highest-risk area?** Try-catch fallback could mask different URL parse errors. Mitigation: second parse only strips whitespace (same operation as before), and SSRF guard still validates the parsed URL.
**How is that risk mitigated?** Protocol check and SSRF validation remain after parsed URL is obtained. Invalid URLs that are not fixed by whitespace removal still throw.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Requesting ClawSweeper re-review.
**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** None — proof updated with live URL parsing demo + real HTTP fetch.

---

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
Co-Authored-By: Claude Code <noreply@anthropic.com>
